headlines.geopolitics: Navy veteran with no family gets full military honors as hundreds refuse to let him be buried alone
headlines.economy: DOJ creates new fraud division to combat 'insane' problem of federal program abuse
headlines.domestic: Fox poll: 87% expect AI to transform lives, but most find it useless today

metadata.daily_do: Check if there's an unclaimed veteran burial happening near you — these ceremonies are always open to the public and they need witnesses

clips.caption: When Pringle explains why we honor unknown soldiers
Duke gets emotional about veteran who died alone
Jax discovers government AI is finding office chairs

script.text: Look, I've been to too many military funerals where the only people there are the honor guard and the chaplain. But when hundreds of strangers show up for a guy they never met? That's America.
Good morning, I'm Leo Barros and this is The Friction. It's Monday, March 10th. Here's what happened while you were sleeping.

First, geopolitics. In Nashville, hundreds of community members and veterans gathered at Middle Tennessee State Veterans Cemetery to give full military honors to Navy veteran Lonnie Wayman, who had no known family to claim his remains.
Oh man.

The VA representative said that while the paperwork called him 'unclaimed,' what unfolded proved that was incorrect. Quote: 'We are able to claim our honorable veterans and provide them the dignity and honor that they have earned.'
That's how it should be.

Economy. Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche announced a new DOJ fraud division to combat what he calls an 'insane' problem of federal program fraud. The initiative targets everything from fake homeless shelters in Minneapolis that received four million in federal funds to non-existent daycare centers billing for school lunches.
Four million for fake homeless shelters? That's like, professional-level scamming.
Blanche says quote, 'no fraud too small, no fraud too large,' and promises anyone stealing over a thousand dollars will face felony charges.
Wait, over a thousand? That's— that's actually pretty low for a federal felony threshold.
Good.

And domestic. A new Fox News poll shows eighty-seven percent of voters expect AI to significantly change American life in coming years, but only forty-seven percent find it useful in their daily lives right now.
So we're all gonna die from robots, but currently they can't even help us find a decent restaurant recommendation.
The poll also found eighty percent of voters are extremely or very concerned about AI eroding trust in news and social media, with most people struggling to identify AI-generated content.
That's terrifying.
We'll dig into all of this after the break. First, though, let's talk about what it means when a community decides no veteran dies alone.
So this story out of Nashville. Lonnie Wayman, Navy veteran, died with no known family. The state was going to bury him as an 'unclaimed' person. Instead, hundreds of people showed up.
Bree, this feels like it connects to larger questions about how we care for veterans, especially isolated ones.
Yeah, I mean, this is beautiful, but it's also heartbreaking that we needed a community mobilization to give this man basic dignity. Like, where were the systems?
The system worked. The community is the system.
But Duke, that's— no, that's not sustainable. We can't rely on viral Facebook posts to make sure veterans get honored. What about the ones who don't get attention?
Look, I've been to military funerals where it's just the chaplain, the honor guard, and maybe a funeral director. It's devastating every single time.
But what happened in Nashville? That's not a policy failure. That's Americans being Americans.
Okay, but what about the systematic issue? We have twenty-two veterans committing suicide every day. Social isolation is a huge part of that.
If Lonnie Wayman died alone with no family connections, what kind of support was he getting while he was alive?
That's a fair question. Duke, do you see room for both? Community response and better veteran services?
Of course. But here's what kills me — we spend billions on veteran programs, and somehow guys like Lonnie still fall through cracks.
Maybe the answer isn't more bureaucracy. Maybe it's more neighbors.
I mean, this is gonna sound dark, but like, how many Lonnie Waymans are there? Veterans dying alone that we never hear about?
That's exactly my point. This story went viral because someone cared enough to organize. But that's random.
It's not random. It's what happens when people remember that service matters. That sacrifice matters.
I'm not arguing against honoring service, Duke. I'm saying we need systems that don't rely on someone happening to care on the right day.
What would that look like practically?

Better tracking of veteran social connections. Proactive outreach programs. Partnership between VA and faith communities. Um, maybe automatic notification systems when a veteran dies without identified family.
You know what? I actually don't hate that last one.
Really?
If we're gonna have a VA database, might as well use it to make sure no one gets buried alone. That's not big government, that's basic respect.
Wait, did Duke just agree with a government program?
I agreed with using existing government infrastructure efficiently. There's a difference.
This feels like one of those stories that reminds us what we're capable of when we remember our obligations to each other.
I just want it to be more than random acts of kindness, you know? I want it to be how we do business.
Fair enough. But sometimes random acts of kindness are exactly what restore your faith in people.
Professor, this connects to something bigger about how societies honor their military dead, doesn't it?
Indeed it does, Leo. What happened in Nashville echoes one of our most sacred military traditions — the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier.

After World War One, nations around the world grappled with an unprecedented problem. Hundreds of thousands of soldiers had died in ways that made identification impossible. Families had no graves to visit, no closure to find.
The solution was brilliant in its simplicity. If we couldn't honor each individual soldier, we would honor one soldier who represented them all.

The British buried their Unknown Warrior at Westminster Abbey in nineteen twenty. The Americans followed suit at Arlington National Cemetery in nineteen twenty-one. France, Belgium, Italy — they all created their own tombs.
So basically everyone was like, 'We need a guy to represent all the guys.'
Precisely, Jax. But here's what's remarkable — these weren't just symbolic gestures. They became focal points for national grief and healing.

When Britain selected their Unknown Warrior, they chose from four unidentified bodies exhumed from the major battlefields. A blindfolded officer placed his hand on one casket. That man became the representative of four hundred thousand British dead.
That's incredibly moving.
The ceremony drew over one million mourners to London. People needed a place to direct their grief, their gratitude, their unfinished conversations with the dead.
That's what happened in Nashville. People needed somewhere to put their respect.

Exactly, Duke. What's fascinating is how these traditions evolved. The Tomb of the Unknown Soldier isn't just about the dead — it's about the living and their need to participate in honoring sacrifice.
At Arlington, the changing of the guard happens every thirty minutes in summer, every hour in winter. Twenty-four hours a day, three hundred sixty-five days a year, since nineteen thirty-seven.
Even during Hurricane Isabel in two thousand three, when Arlington was evacuated, the guards refused to leave their post.
Wait, they stayed during a hurricane?
They did. The sergeant of the guard said, quote, 'Soldiers have stood guard over the Tomb through much worse than a little wind and rain.' End quote.
That's the point, isn't it? The consistency. The reliability. Someone is always watching.
Precisely. In Nashville, hundreds of people became temporary guardians for Lonnie Wayman. They stood watch, if only for one morning.
So the tradition isn't just about the individual soldier. It's about us — the living — proving we remember.
Beautifully put, Bree. The Unknown Soldier represents all the unknown soldiers. But more than that, he represents our commitment to never let service go unacknowledged.
Professor, thank you. That puts this Nashville story in a much larger context.
History doesn't repeat, Leo, but human decency certainly rhymes.
Alright, time for rapid fire, where we find out what else is happening in our beautiful, chaotic world. And folks, today's stories prove that reality has completely given up trying to make sense.
First up: Palantir's AI weapons system is apparently now being used to find office chairs for government workers. I'm not kidding.
I'm sorry, what?
The same artificial intelligence that was designed to identify terrorist threats is now optimizing federal furniture procurement. From finding bombs to finding ergonomic lumbar support.
This is either the most efficient government innovation ever or the most expensive way to buy chairs in human history.
I have so many questions.
Next story: A boss in Ireland repeatedly shouted 'potato' at his Irish employee and apparently thought this was normal workplace communication. The employee won a harassment case.
Potato?
Just, like, 'Potato! Hey, potato!' as a form of address. For months. The boss apparently thought this was hilarious.
That's not even creative racism. That's just lazy racism.
The potato famine jokes never stopped being offensive, they just stopped being clever in eighteen forty-seven.
Oh snap, Professor with the historical smackdown! Okay, next: Louisiana schools have until Friday to post the Ten Commandments in every classroom, as required by new state law.
I'm sure that won't cause any lawsuits.
The ACLU is probably already writing the briefs. This is going straight to the Supreme Court.
And finally, in Florida — because of course it's Florida — a drug dealer locked a man in a storage unit for months over an unpaid debt. The man survived on snacks and bottled water.
For months?
Months. Like, this wasn't a weekend timeout. This was a whole lifestyle. I'm picturing him rating different brands of beef jerky to pass the time.
How do you not escape from a storage unit? Those things are basically cardboard.
Maybe he was afraid of getting shot if he tried? This is a kidnapping, not an escape room.
Fair point. Although 'Florida Drug Dealer Escape Room' sounds like the worst theme park attraction ever conceived.
And that's rapid fire, where we learn that AI finds office chairs, bosses yell vegetables, Louisiana posts commandments, and Florida remains Florida.
The more things change, the more Florida stays exactly the same.
Alright, time for today's Daily Do. After hearing about Lonnie Wayman's funeral, here's something you can actually act on.

Check if there's an unclaimed veteran burial happening near you. These ceremonies are always open to the public, and they need witnesses. A quick Google search for 'unclaimed veteran burial' plus your city name will usually turn up information.
Most happen with very little notice — sometimes just a few days. But showing up matters. It transforms a bureaucratic procedure into an act of community remembrance.
And you don't need to know the person. You just need to know that service deserves recognition.
It's also a good way to connect with local veteran organizations if you want to get more involved in veteran support.
Exactly. Sometimes the most meaningful thing you can do is simply show up and stand quietly while someone gets the honor they earned.
As the Roman historian Tacitus wrote, 'The desire for glory clings even to the best men longer than any other passion.' Even in death, we seek recognition for our service.
Professor, thank you. That's The Friction for Monday, March tenth. I'm Leo Barros.
I'm Jax Mooney, reminding you that somewhere in America, an AI is probably optimizing the procurement of staplers as we speak.
We'll see you tomorrow.
